meta.title: Process Transformation Accelerator — Navigation Spine
meta.tagline: From client mission to running agent — one continuous pipeline.
meta.note: Function-agnostic. Order-to-Cash is fully built; other functions are samples that prove the framework generalises.

stages.name: Design
stages.blurb: Pick the mission, choose the patterns, configure the action blocks.

stages.name: Discover & Fit
stages.blurb: Assess every action block for agent fit; record and agentivise the happy path.

stages.name: Run & Govern
stages.blurb: Straight-through on the happy path; variations matched to patterns and routed for approval.

themes.name: Working Capital Optimization
themes.tagline: Collect faster, pay smarter, deploy idle cash — compress the cash conversion cycle.

crossProcessPatterns.name: Bilateral Cash Squeeze
crossProcessPatterns.description: Customer delays payment (DSO up) while supplier demands early payment (DPO down). Working capital squeezed from both sides simultaneously.
triggers: Early Pay Discount pressure in P2P
crossProcessPatterns.compoundImpact: 2x working capital erosion vs. either signal alone
crossProcessPatterns.response: Synchronized collections push + supplier payment renegotiation

crossProcessPatterns.name: Quarter-End Cash Cliff
crossProcessPatterns.description: Customers ghost at quarter-end (O2C Pattern #30) while supplier payments concentrate in same period.
crossProcessPatterns.compoundImpact: Cash position drops 40-60% in final 5 business days of quarter
crossProcessPatterns.response: Pre-emptive collection calls week 3 of quarter + supplier payment staggering

themes.name: Revenue Leakage Prevention
themes.tagline: Stop money walking out the door through disputes, deductions, write-offs, and pricing errors.

compoundKPI.name: Revenue Leakage Rate
compoundKPI.formula: (Disputes Written Off + Invalid Deductions + Bad Debt + Refund Fraud) / Gross Revenue

crossProcessPatterns.name: Bust-Out Composite Signal
crossProcessPatterns.description: Customer simultaneously: gaming dispute timing (Collections), requesting duplicate refunds (Cash App), credit score dropping (Credit). Individual signals are flags — together it is bust-out fraud.
crossProcessPatterns.response: Immediate credit freeze + legal hold + CFO notification

themes.name: Financial Close Acceleration
themes.tagline: Compress close from 10 days to 4. Every day saved is a day for analysis instead of reconciliation.

compoundKPI.name: Close Cycle Days
compoundKPI.formula: Day of final GL posting - Day 1 of close

crossProcessPatterns.name: Unapplied Cash Close Block
crossProcessPatterns.description: Unapplied cash from Cash App blocks AR subledger reconciliation in R2R, which blocks trial balance, which delays close.
crossProcessPatterns.compoundImpact: Each $1M unapplied cash adds ~0.5 days to close cycle
crossProcessPatterns.response: Auto-match waterfall runs Day -3 before close; exceptions routed to Cash App team with 24-hour SLA

themes.name: Compliance & Controls Integrity
themes.tagline: Zero material weaknesses. Embedded controls, continuous monitoring, audit-ready always.

crossProcessPatterns.name: Override Cascade
crossProcessPatterns.description: Sales rep overrides credit hold (Pattern #29) on a customer who is already exploiting dispute process (Pattern #1). Two control failures compound: customer gets goods on credit while delaying payment on existing invoices.
crossProcessPatterns.compoundImpact: SOX finding: segregation of duties failure + credit risk exposure
crossProcessPatterns.response: Block override if customer has active dispute manipulation pattern; auto-escalate to Credit VP

themes.name: Cash Visibility & Forecasting
themes.tagline: See your cash 13 weeks out with 90%+ accuracy. Predict customer payment behavior, not just due dates.

crossProcessPatterns.name: Payment Behavior Drift
crossProcessPatterns.description: Pattern #27 (Creeping Payment Terms) + Pattern #31 (Chronic Promise Breaker) together predict that a customer segment's actual payment date is drifting 2-3 days per quarter. Standard forecast models using contractual terms miss this.
crossProcessPatterns.compoundImpact: Forecast error of $2-5M per quarter from payment timing drift
crossProcessPatterns.response: Override forecast model with behavioral prediction for flagged customers

functions.name: Order-to-Cash
functions.tagline: AR collections, disputes, credit risk — the fully-built core.

areas.name: Collections Mgmt
sub: Promise to pay / payment plan capture

processes.name: AR Collections & Dispute Management
processes.desc: Behavioural collections: read intent, sequence the right action, escalate only when warranted.

processes.name: Sales Order Processing & Credit Control
processes.desc: The front of the cycle: clean orders and disciplined credit so problems never enter billing or collections downstream.

processes.name: Billing & Dispute Management
processes.desc: Bill it right the first time, and resolve the disputes that slip through - the two sides of invoice integrity.

objectives.name: Bill accurately, on time, first time
Resolve disputes within SLA, maximise recovery

processes.name: Cash Application & Deductions
processes.desc: Apply every payment and recover every invalid deduction - lift auto-match and stop leakage at the point of cash.

objectives.name: Recover invalid deductions, find the leakage root

processes.name: AR Operations & Working Capital
processes.desc: The supervisory view: steer DSO, govern provisions and master data, and catch compound issues that span every AR sub-process.

functions.name: Procure-to-Pay
functions.tagline: Invoice integrity, matching, and payment timing that protects cash.

processes.name: AP Invoice Processing & Working Capital
processes.desc: From invoice receipt to payment — duplicates, ghost vendors, matching integrity, and payment timing that protects cash.

functions.name: Record-to-Report
functions.tagline: A faster, cleaner close — reconciliations, accruals, intercompany.

processes.name: Financial Close & Reconciliation
processes.desc: Period-end: clear breaks, post accruals, balance intercompany.

functions.name: Hire-to-Retire
functions.tagline: Onboarding that's ready on day one, compliantly.

processes.name: Employee Onboarding
processes.desc: From offer-accept to productive day-one — provisioning and compliance.

processes.name: Order Fulfillment & Replenishment
processes.desc: Keep shelves stocked and promises kept — stockouts and slippage.

functions.name: Financial Planning & Analysis
functions.tagline: Rolling forecast, variance insight, and decision support — the perform half of finance.

processes.name: Forecasting & Variance Management
processes.desc: Keep the forecast honest and the variances explained — bias out, insight in.

objectives.name: Keep the rolling forecast accurate & bias-free

functions.name: Treasury & Cash Management
functions.tagline: Cash visibility, liquidity, and FX / covenant risk — money in motion.

processes.name: Cash & Liquidity Management
processes.desc: See the cash, sweep the idle, and catch the risk before it's a surprise.

functions.name: Tax Management
functions.tagline: Determination, compliance, and provisioning — accurate, penalty-free tax.

processes.name: Indirect Tax Compliance & Filing
processes.desc: File on time and right — reconcile to the ledger, determine at source, never miss a deadline.

functions.name: Travel & Expense
functions.tagline: Expense capture, policy, and reimbursement — spend that behaves.

processes.name: Expense Audit & Compliance
processes.desc: Catch the leakage and keep reimbursement frictionless — audit smart, pay clean.

functions.name: Internal Controls & Compliance
functions.tagline: Continuous control monitoring and segregation of duties — trust, evidenced.

processes.name: Continuous Control Monitoring & SoD
processes.desc: Catch control failures and toxic access before they become losses — assurance, continuously.

2002.name: Duplicate Invoice Injection
2002.mentalModel: The 'new' invoice is last month's bill wearing a disguise — a tweaked PO reference and a rounded total, but the same vendor and the same economic amount. Pay both and we've bought the same thing twice. I match on the fingerprint, not the invoice number.

sap: RBKP (invoice receipt header)
BSEG (posted payables)
EKPO (PO line reference)
oracle: AP_INVOICES_ALL (invoice header)
AP_INVOICE_LINES_ALL (line detail)

2002.layer1_logicalMapping: Vendor + net amount + baseline date on the incoming invoice, fingerprinted and matched against every posted and open payable to catch a re-bill that changed only its surface identifiers.
2002.layer2_eventSeries: Invoice arrival → fingerprint(vendor+amount+date) → match against posted & open items → exact vs. fuzzy (rounded amount / alternate PO) → block or clear before payment.
2002.conceptualSQL:
-- pre-calculated trigger for Duplicate_Fingerprint
SELECT * FROM ap_signals WHERE signal = 'Duplicate_Fingerprint'

2003.name: Ghost Vendor
2003.mentalModel: A vendor master appears, invoices start flowing, but there's no goods receipt and no service entry behind any of them — and the bank details were added the same week the first invoice landed. No receipt, fresh master, new bank: that's a ghost vendor until someone proves otherwise.

sap: LFA1 (vendor master + creation date)
RBKP (invoice receipt)
EKKO (PO / goods-receipt link)
oracle: AP_SUPPLIERS (supplier master)
AP_INVOICES_ALL (invoice header)
RCV_TRANSACTIONS (receipts)

2003.layer1_logicalMapping: Each invoice is joined to its goods-receipt / service-entry evidence and to the age of the vendor master and its bank record; invoices with no receipt behind a freshly-created master are held.
2003.layer2_eventSeries: Vendor master created → invoices submitted → check for matching GR / service entry → cross-check master & bank-record age → no receipt + new master + new bank ⇒ freeze.
2003.conceptualSQL:
-- pre-calculated trigger for Ghost_Vendor
SELECT * FROM ap_signals WHERE signal = 'Ghost_Vendor'

branchingDAG.condition: ELSE IF No_Receipt AND New_Master AND New_Bank

2006.name: Three-Way Match Override Abuse
2006.mentalModel: The GR/IR quantity mismatch keeps getting force-released by the same clerk with no note — same vendor, same override, every month. One override is judgement; a habit of overrides with no investigation is a control hole. I re-open the pattern, not just the single invoice.

sap: RBKP (invoice / block-release)
EKPO (PO vs GR quantities)
BSEG (posted amount)
oracle: AP_HOLDS_ALL (holds & releases)
AP_INVOICES_ALL (invoice header)
RCV_TRANSACTIONS (receipts)

2006.layer1_logicalMapping: Every match block release is attributed to a user and vendor and tested for a recurring override with no attached investigation evidence — the signature of a rubber-stamped control.
2006.layer2_eventSeries: GR/IR mismatch → block → who released it and how often → is there investigation evidence → repeat override with none ⇒ re-impose block and route to controls.
2006.conceptualSQL:
-- pre-calculated trigger for ThreeWay_Override
SELECT * FROM ap_signals WHERE signal = 'ThreeWay_Override'

2001.name: Early Pay Discount Trap
2001.mentalModel: The 2/10 net-30 says pay by day 10 for the discount — but the payment run grabs it on autopilot even when our own cash is tight and the discount doesn't beat our cost of cash. I only take the early pay when the math actually works.

sap: EKKO (payment terms ZTERM)
RBKP (invoice header, baseline date)
BSEG (open payables, discount fields)
oracle: AP_TERMS (discount terms)
AP_PAYMENT_SCHEDULES_ALL (schedule)
AP_INVOICES_ALL (invoice header)

2001.layer1_logicalMapping: Discount terms and baseline date, joined to the projected cash position on the proposed pay date, decide whether taking the early-payment discount earns more than holding the cash.
2001.layer2_eventSeries: Discount window opens → payment-run selection date vs. day-10 deadline → cash position on that date → captured discount value vs. cost of cash → pay early only when it wins.
2001.conceptualSQL:
-- pre-calculated trigger for Early_Pay_Misfire
SELECT * FROM ap_signals WHERE signal = 'Early_Pay_Misfire'

branchingDAG.hitl: Treasury approves early pay vs. hold

hitlGates: Treasury approves early pay vs. hold

2004.name: PO Split Below Approval Threshold
2004.mentalModel: One requisition became three POs to the same vendor in the same week, each landing just under the approval limit. Individually each auto-approves; together it's a decision someone senior should sign. I aggregate the window before releasing any of them.

sap: EBAN (purchase requisition)
EKKO (PO header)
EKPO (PO item)
oracle: POR_REQUISITION_HEADERS_ALL (requisitions)
PO_HEADERS_ALL (PO header)
PO_LINES_ALL (PO lines)

2004.layer1_logicalMapping: POs are aggregated by vendor, requester and rolling window and compared against the approval tiers to catch a spend deliberately sliced to stay under each individual gate.
2004.layer2_eventSeries: Requisition → multiple POs to one vendor in a window → aggregate value → does the total cross an approval tier that no single PO did → re-tier before release.
2004.conceptualSQL:
-- pre-calculated trigger for Split_Under_Threshold
SELECT * FROM ap_signals WHERE signal = 'Split_Under_Threshold'

2005.name: Retroactive Price Change
2005.mentalModel: The PO was approved at one unit price; the invoice matches a price that was quietly edited on the PO after approval but before matching. The three-way match passes because the PO was changed to fit the invoice — I check the price against the approved version, not the current one.

sap: EKPO (PO item price + change history)
EKKO (PO header)
RBKP (invoice receipt)
oracle: PO_LINES_ALL (PO line price)
PO_LINE_LOCATIONS_ALL (shipments)
AP_INVOICE_LINES_ALL (invoice lines)

2005.layer1_logicalMapping: The invoice price is matched against the PO price as approved (from change history), not the current PO value, so a post-approval edit that silently lifts the price is caught before it clears.
2005.layer2_eventSeries: PO approved at price P → PO line price edited after approval → invoice matches the edited price → compare against approved P → block increases beyond tolerance.
2005.conceptualSQL:
-- pre-calculated trigger for Retro_Price_Change
SELECT * FROM ap_signals WHERE signal = 'Retro_Price_Change'

2007.name: Payment Term Erosion
2007.mentalModel: Contract says net-45 but we're actually paying this vendor on day 30 — the effective DPO has drifted a week short of what we negotiated. Paying early for no discount is just giving working capital away. I catch the drift and hold to terms.

sap: BSEG (clearing / payment dates)
EKKO (contracted terms ZTERM)
LFA1 (vendor master terms)
oracle: AP_PAYMENT_SCHEDULES_ALL (payment dates)
AP_INVOICES_ALL (invoice header)
AP_TERMS (terms)

2007.layer1_logicalMapping: Effective DPO per vendor is computed from actual clearing dates and compared to the contracted terms to surface systematic early payment that surrenders working capital for no discount.
2007.layer2_eventSeries: Actual payment dates → effective DPO by vendor → compare to contract terms → early with no discount benefit ⇒ reschedule to net due; systemic ⇒ treasury.
2007.conceptualSQL:
-- pre-calculated trigger for DPO_Erosion
SELECT * FROM ap_signals WHERE signal = 'DPO_Erosion'

2008.name: Supplier Concentration Risk
2008.mentalModel: One supplier now takes more than 40% of this category's spend. That's convenient until they raise prices or fail — concentration quietly hands them the leverage. I surface it while there's still time to qualify a second source.

sap: EKKO (PO spend by vendor)
EKPO (category / material group)
LFA1 (vendor master)
oracle: PO_HEADERS_ALL (PO header)
PO_LINES_ALL (category spend)
POZ_SUPPLIERS (supplier master)

2008.layer1_logicalMapping: Category spend is aggregated by vendor to compute each supplier's share; a share above policy flags a concentration risk before pricing power or a supplier failure becomes the company's problem.
2008.layer2_eventSeries: Spend by category and vendor → concentration share → above 40% with alternatives ⇒ dual-source; sole-source or above 60% ⇒ escalate a risk assessment.
2008.conceptualSQL:
-- pre-calculated trigger for Supplier_Concentration
SELECT * FROM ap_signals WHERE signal = 'Supplier_Concentration'

3001.name: Stuck Reconciliation Item
3001.mentalModel: This GL-to-subledger break has rolled forward three closes. Is it a timing item that will clear, or a real difference someone must own? Age it and route.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

3001.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.
3001.conceptualSQL:
-- sample trigger for Aging_Recon_Break
SELECT * FROM source WHERE signal = 'Aging_Recon_Break'

3004.name: Bank Reconciliation Outlier
3004.mentalModel: A bank line has sat unmatched for two weeks — not an in-transit item, it's stopped clearing. Either a payment failed silently or something posted twice. I chase the outlier now, before it's buried in next month's noise.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

3004.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.
3004.conceptualSQL:

SELECT * FROM source WHERE signal = 'Bank_Recon_Outlier'

3005.name: Suspense Account Creep
3005.mentalModel: The suspense and clearing accounts are quietly filling up — items land there 'temporarily' and never leave. A growing suspense balance is unreconciled risk in disguise; I age it and force each item to a home.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

3005.layer1_logicalMapping: Sample logical mapping for the Suspense Account Creep pattern.
3005.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.
3005.conceptualSQL:
-- sample trigger for Suspense_Aging
SELECT * FROM source WHERE signal = 'Suspense_Aging'

3006.name: Auto-Certified Reconciliation
3006.mentalModel: This account was certified 'reconciled' in four seconds flat, same as every month — but the supporting balance hasn't moved and nobody attached evidence. A rubber-stamped rec isn't a control; I re-open the risky ones for a real look.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

3006.layer1_logicalMapping: Sample logical mapping for the Auto-Certified Reconciliation pattern.
3006.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.
3006.conceptualSQL:

SELECT * FROM source WHERE signal = 'RubberStamp_Recon'

3002.name: Late / Missing Accrual
3002.mentalModel: A recurring accrual that posted every month is missing this period. Did the activity stop, or did someone forget? Detect the gap before the books close.

3003.name: Intercompany Out-of-Balance
3003.mentalModel: Entity A booked the charge; Entity B never picked it up. Every day this lingers it distorts the consolidation. Match the legs and chase the missing one.

oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

3007.name: Close Task Critical-Path Slip
3007.mentalModel: A close task just slipped and it's on the critical path — every downstream task is now waiting on it. I don't need a status meeting; I need to know who the one bottleneck is and nudge them before the whole close moves a day right.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

3007.layer1_logicalMapping: Sample logical mapping for the Close Task Critical-Path Slip pattern.
3007.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.
3007.conceptualSQL:
-- sample trigger for Close_CriticalPath
SELECT * FROM source WHERE signal = 'Close_CriticalPath'

3008.name: Period Cut-Off Error
3008.mentalModel: A big invoice for in-period services posted two days after the cut-off, into the next month. Booked in the wrong period it distorts both months' results. I catch cut-off errors at close, not when the auditor does.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

3008.layer1_logicalMapping: Sample logical mapping for the Period Cut-Off Error pattern.
3008.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.
3008.conceptualSQL:

4001.name: Stalled Onboarding Provisioning
4001.mentalModel: Day-one is in 48 hours and laptop, accounts, and badge are still 'pending'. Which task is the bottleneck, and who do I nudge before the new hire shows up to nothing?

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

4003.name: Manager Onboarding No-Show
4003.mentalModel: Day-one is 48 hours out and the hiring manager still hasn't done a single onboarding task — no buddy, no equipment sign-off, no first-week plan. The new hire will show up to a manager who forgot they were coming. I nudge the manager before the impression is set.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

4003.layer1_logicalMapping: Sample logical mapping for the Manager Onboarding No-Show pattern.
4003.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.
4003.conceptualSQL:

SELECT * FROM source WHERE signal = 'Manager_Prep_Gap'

4004.name: Access Not Ready Day-1
4004.mentalModel: Laptop's here, but the accounts — email, VPN, the core app — still aren't provisioned and start is tomorrow. A new hire who can't log in on day one loses their first week and their first impression. I chase the access owner, not just the asset.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

4004.layer1_logicalMapping: Sample logical mapping for the Access Not Ready Day-1 pattern.
4004.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.
4004.conceptualSQL:
-- sample trigger for Access_Provisioning_Gap
SELECT * FROM source WHERE signal = 'Access_Provisioning_Gap'

4005.name: Ghost-Start Risk
4005.mentalModel: The new hire has gone quiet — didn't confirm start details, didn't open the pre-boarding portal, and it's a hot market. Ghosting after accept is real, and every provisioned laptop and licence is sunk cost if they no-show. I re-engage now and warn the manager to keep a backup warm.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

4005.layer1_logicalMapping: Sample logical mapping for the Ghost-Start Risk pattern.
4005.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.

4002.name: Missing Compliance Document
4002.mentalModel: Right-to-work paperwork still isn't on file and the start date is locked. Chase it now, or we have a compliance exposure on day one.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

4002.layer1_logicalMapping: Sample logical mapping for the Missing Compliance Document pattern.
4002.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.

4006.name: Background-Check Stall
4006.mentalModel: The background check has been 'in progress' for two weeks and the start date is locked. Let them start before it clears and we've got an unvetted person in a sensitive role; hold it blindly and we lose the hire. I chase the vendor and prep the conditional-start call.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

4006.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.

4007.name: Expiring Work Authorization
4007.mentalModel: This hire's work permit expires four months after they start — right in the middle of probation. An expiring authorization nobody's tracking becomes an illegal-to-employ problem overnight. I flag the renewal runway now, while there's still time to act.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

4008.mentalModel: This role legally requires a professional licence, and the one on file can't be verified against the register — maybe lapsed, maybe never held. Putting an unlicensed person in a regulated seat is a fine and a headline. I verify at source before day one.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

4008.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.

5001.name: Stockout Risk Signal
5001.mentalModel: Demand is trending up and on-hand cover just dropped below lead time. Do I expedite, substitute, or ride it out? Decide before the shelf goes empty.

6000.name: Budget Sandbagging
6000.mentalModel: The regional budget landed 15% below current run-rate — right after their bonus was tied to budget attainment. That's not conservatism, it's a sandbag: lowball the plan so any outcome looks like a beat.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

6000.layer1_logicalMapping: Sample logical mapping for the Budget Sandbagging pattern.
6000.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.
6000.conceptualSQL:
-- sample trigger for Sandbag_Signal
SELECT * FROM source WHERE signal = 'Sandbag_Signal'

6001.name: Hockey-Stick Forecast
6001.mentalModel: Every quarter the forecast crawls flat then miraculously spikes in the final month to hit the number. The back-loaded hockey stick almost never lands — I discount the miracle month before it's baked in.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

6001.layer1_logicalMapping: Sample logical mapping for the Hockey-Stick Forecast pattern.
6001.layer2_eventSeries: Sample event series derived from the source timeline.
6001.conceptualSQL:
-- sample trigger for BackLoaded_Curve
SELECT * FROM source WHERE signal = 'BackLoaded_Curve'

6002.name: Forecast Drift
6002.mentalModel: The forecast still assumes 8% growth three months after the market turned. Nobody refreshed the driver — the gap between what we're steering by and reality is widening silently.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)

6002.layer1_logicalMapping: Sample logical mapping for the Forecast Drift pattern.

6002.conceptualSQL:
-- sample trigger for Assumption_Staleness
SELECT * FROM source WHERE signal = 'Assumption_Staleness'

6003.name: Reforecast Trigger Miss
6003.mentalModel: A material event just hit — a lost contract, an FX swing — but no reforecast was triggered. The number the business is steering by is already wrong and nobody's noticed.

sap: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
oracle: (sample mapping — connect your system of record)
